attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with three months of increasing fatigue, night sweats, and early satiety. She has lost 5 kg in weight over this period. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears pale. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is a firm, non-tender spleen palpable 8 cm below the costal margin. No lymphadenopathy is detected.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 98 g/L (115-165)

  • White cell count 22 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelets 450 x 10^9/L (150-400)

  • Lactate dehydrogenase 850 U/L (120-250)

Blood film shows many teardrop poikilocytes, nucleated red blood cells, occasional myelocytes and blasts.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of primary myelofibrosis, including constitutional symptoms (fatigue, night sweats, weight loss), marked splenomegaly, and characteristic peripheral blood findings. The blood film shows a leukoerythroblastic picture (presence of both nucleated red blood cells and myeloid precursors) with teardrop cells, which is typical of myelofibrosis. The raised lactate dehydrogenase reflects increased cell turnover.

Primary myelofibrosis is the most likely diagnosis given the combination of constitutional symptoms, significant splenomegaly, and characteristic blood film findings. A bone marrow biopsy is required to confirm the diagnosis, and a "dry tap" is commonly encountered where no aspirate can be obtained.

Chronic myeloid leukaemia typically presents with a much higher white cell count (often >100 x 10^9/L). The blood film also typically shows a left shift with all maturation stages seen, basophilia and eosinophilia. While splenomegaly can occur, the blood film findings are different from this case.

Essential thrombocythaemia typically presents with a much higher platelet count (>450 x 10^9/L) and does not usually cause significant splenomegaly or show a leukoerythroblastic blood picture.

Acute myeloid leukaemia typically presents more acutely with high blast count (>20%), severe cytopenias, and often with bleeding or infection. While splenomegaly can occur, the characteristic teardrop cells and leukoerythroblastic picture are not typical features.

Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia typically presents with lymphocytosis and lymphadenopathy. While splenomegaly can occur, the blood film would show mature-appearing lymphocytes rather than the myeloid precursors and nucleated red blood cells seen here.

The diagnosis would be confirmed with bone marrow biopsy showing increased reticulin fibrosis, and molecular testing for JAK2, CALR, and MPL mutations, which are present in approximately 90% of cases.
learningPoints: Primary myelofibrosis presents with constitutional symptoms, splenomegaly, and characteristic peripheral blood findings including teardrop cells and leukoerythroblastic picture.
JAK2 V617F mutation is present in approximately 60% of primary myelofibrosis cases.
The combination of splenomegaly, constitutional symptoms and leukoerythroblastic blood film is highly suggestive of primary myelofibrosis.
references: Passamonti F, Mora B. Myelofibrosis. Blood. 2023;141(16):1954-1970. doi:10.1182/blood.2022017423
